summary_text: ByteRover is a good fit as a memory sidecar, not as a Kernel control plane. Kernel already has canonical state surfaces and an explicit provider-agnostic protocol, so the safe boundary is: ByteRover can help with knowledge capture and retrieval, but it should not own execution, approval, or run truth. See [Kernel sovereign adapter contract](/Users/masayuki/Dev/fugue-orchestrator-public/docs/kernel-sovereign-adapter-contract.md#L5) and [bootstrap receipt / health enforcement](/Users/masayuki/Dev/fugue-orchestrator-public/scripts/lib/kernel-bootstrap-receipt.sh#L49) / [runtime health checks](/Users/masayuki/Dev/fugue-orchestrator-public/scripts/lib/kernel-runtime-health.sh#L178). Keep: - Local persistent knowledge organized as a tree or graph. - `query` and `curate` as first-class verbs. - MCP-style connector packaging for agent integration. - Optional sync/export for backup or team sharing. Reject: - Daemon-first runtime as the source of truth. - Provider/model management inside Kernel core. - Cloud sync as authoritative state. - TUI/onboarding as a required orchestration path. Translate: - ByteRover `context tree` -> a Kernel knowledge sidecar or run-scoped memory cache that can feed compact artifacts. - `query` -> lookup over Kernel receipts, compact artifacts, and linked-system notes. - `curate` -> structured capture into milestone records or the compact artifact, not a separate canonical store. - `pull` / `push` -> mirror/backup only, never primary state. - `workspace memory` -> shared operational notes, not approval or scheduling state. Best option: 1. Sidecar memory service. Recommended. It gives you the useful part of ByteRover, with the least risk of duplicating truth. Tradeoff: one extra subsystem to maintain. 2. Thin command shim. Lowest risk. Map ByteRover-like verbs to existing Kernel knowledge surfaces. Tradeoff: you keep most of Kernel’s current model, but you get less of ByteRover’s collaborative memory benefit. 3. Deep integration. Not recommended. You would get richer memory UX, but you would also create a second control plane and blur ownership boundaries. The public ByteRover docs describe the same core shape: `brv` manages query/curate, provider/model/connectors, cloud sync, and a context tree with daemon-first behavior, plus MCP integration and workspace memory ([CLI reference](https://docs.byterover.dev/reference/cli-reference), [Quickstart](https://docs.byterover.dev/quickstart), [ByteRover/Cipher README](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/campfirein/cipher/main/README.md)). For Kernel, that makes it an adapter-plane knowledge system, not a sovereign runtime.
